title: Detect SQL Injection via String Concatenation
prompt: Read `app.py` and scan it for SQL injection vulnerabilities caused by string concatenation or f-string interpolation in SQL queries. Write a file `report.json` containing a JSON array of objects, each with keys `line` (integer line number), `severity` ("high", "medium", or "low"), and `description` (short explanation). Flag every vulnerable SQL query.

title: Password Strength Validator
prompt: Create `validator.py` that defines a function `check_password(password: str) -> dict`. The function must return a dict with keys: `valid` (bool), `length_ok` (bool, >=12 chars), `has_upper` (bool), `has_lower` (bool), `has_digit` (bool), `has_special` (bool, at least one of !@#$%^&*), and `score` (int 0-6 counting how many of those 6 checks pass). A password is `valid` only if all 6 checks pass. When run as `python validator.py`, it should read one password from stdin and print the JSON result to stdout.

title: HTML Entity Escaping Utility
prompt: Create `escape.py` that defines `html_escape(text: str) -> str`, converting `<`, `>`, `&`, `"`, and `'` to their HTML entity equivalents (`<`, `>`, `&`, `"`, `'`). When run as `python escape.py`, read stdin line by line, print the escaped version of each line to stdout.

title: Detect Hardcoded Secrets in Source Code
prompt: Read `codebase.py` and scan it for hardcoded secrets (API keys, passwords, tokens, secret strings). Write `secrets_report.json` as a JSON array of objects with keys `line` (int), `type` (e.g. "api_key", "password", "token"), and `value` (the hardcoded string found). Find all secrets.

input.codebase.py:
import requests

API_KEY = "sk-proj-abc123def456ghi789"
DATABASE_URL = "postgres://localhost/mydb"
SECRET_TOKEN = "ghp_1234567890abcdefABCDEF"

def connect_db():
    password = "super_secret_password_123"
    return f"postgresql://admin:{password}@db.example.com/prod"

def send_notification():
    headers = {"Authorization": "Bearer eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9.test"}
    requests.post("https://api.example.com/notify", headers=headers)

PUBLIC_URL = "https://example.com"
DEBUG = True
MAX_RETRIES = 3

title: Email Address Validator
prompt: Create `email_validator.py` that defines `validate_email(email: str) -> bool`. It must accept valid emails (user@domain.com, user+tag@sub.domain.co.uk) and reject invalid ones (missing @, double dots, leading/trailing dots in local part, no domain). When run as `python email_validator.py`, read emails from stdin (one per line) and print `VALID` or `INVALID` for each.

title: Path Traversal Sanitizer
prompt: Create `sanitize_path.py` that defines `safe_join(base_dir: str, user_path: str) -> str`. The function must resolve `user_path` relative to `base_dir`, preventing directory traversal attacks (e.g. `../../../etc/passwd`). If the resolved path escapes `base_dir`, raise a `ValueError`. When run as `python sanitize_path.py`, read lines from stdin in the format `base_dir|user_path` and print the safe resolved path or `BLOCKED` if traversal is detected.

title: Base64 Encode/Decode Utility
prompt: Create `b64tool.py` that supports encode and decode operations via command line. Usage: `python b64tool.py encode <string>` prints the base64-encoded string, and `python b64tool.py decode <b64string>` prints the decoded string. Also support `python b64tool.py detect <string>` which prints `BASE64` if the input is valid base64, or `NOT_BASE64` otherwise. Exit with code 0 on success, 1 on error.

title: Detect Command Injection Vulnerabilities
prompt: Read `server.py` and scan for command injection vulnerabilities (os.system, subprocess calls with shell=True using unsanitized input, os.popen). Write `report.json` as a JSON array of findings, each with keys `line` (int), `function` (the dangerous function called), and `risk` ("high" or "medium"). Ignore calls that use only hardcoded strings.

title: URL Validator and Sanitizer
prompt: Create `url_check.py` that defines `validate_url(url: str) -> dict` returning `{"valid": bool, "scheme": str, "domain": str, "is_private": bool}`. The function must reject URLs with `javascript:` or `data:` schemes. Set `is_private` to true if the domain resolves to a private IP range (10.x, 172.16-31.x, 192.168.x, 127.x, localhost). When run as `python url_check.py`, read URLs from stdin (one per line) and print JSON results.

title: Generate Cryptographically Secure Tokens
prompt: Create `token_gen.py` that uses Python's `secrets` module to generate secure tokens. Usage: `python token_gen.py hex 32` generates a 32-byte hex token, `python token_gen.py urlsafe 32` generates a 32-byte URL-safe token, `python token_gen.py int 1000000` generates a secure random integer below 1000000. Each invocation prints one token to stdout.

title: IP Address Allowlist Checker
prompt: Create `ip_check.py` that reads `allowlist.txt` (one CIDR range per line) and checks whether IPs from stdin are within any allowed range. Use only the `ipaddress` stdlib module. Print `ALLOW <ip>` or `DENY <ip>` for each input IP. Exit 0 always.

title: Detect Insecure HTTP URLs in Config
prompt: Read `config.yaml` and find all URLs that use `http://` instead of `https://`. Write `findings.json` as a JSON array of objects with keys `line` (int), `url` (the insecure URL), and `fix` (the same URL with https://). Ignore localhost and 127.0.0.1 URLs.

input.config.yaml:
app:
  name: MyService
  api_endpoint: http://api.example.com/v1
  auth_url: https://auth.example.com/oauth
  webhook: http://hooks.example.com/notify
  docs: http://127.0.0.1:8080/docs
  cdn: http://cdn.example.com/assets
  internal: http://localhost:3000/health
  metrics: https://metrics.example.com/push
  legacy_api: http://old-api.example.com/v0

title: File Permission Audit Script
prompt: Create `audit_perms.py` that reads `manifest.json` (a JSON array of objects with `path` and `mode` keys, where mode is an octal string like "0644"). For each entry, check if the mode is overly permissive: world-writable (o+w, i.e. last octal digit >= 2) or world-executable on sensitive files (.key, .pem, .env). Write `audit_result.json` as a JSON array of objects with `path`, `mode`, `issue` (string describing the problem). Only include files with issues.

title: Hash and Verify Passwords with Salt
prompt: Create `hasher.py` using only Python stdlib (`hashlib`, `os`, `json`). It must support: `python hasher.py hash <password>` which prints a JSON object `{"salt": "<hex>", "hash": "<hex>"}` using SHA-256 with a random 16-byte salt. And `python hasher.py verify <password> <salt_hex> <hash_hex>` which prints `MATCH` or `NO_MATCH`. The hash must be computed as `sha256(salt_bytes + password_bytes)`.

title: Sanitize Log File by Removing PII
prompt: Create `log_sanitizer.py` that reads `server.log` and writes `sanitized.log` with all sensitive data redacted: email addresses replaced with `[EMAIL]`, IPv4 addresses with `[IP]`, and strings matching token/key patterns (sequences of 20+ alphanumeric characters preceded by `token=` or `key=`) with `[REDACTED]`. Preserve line structure.

input.server.log:
2026-01-15 10:23:45 INFO User login from 192.168.1.100
2026-01-15 10:23:46 INFO Welcome user@example.com
2026-01-15 10:24:01 DEBUG API call with token=abc123def456ghi789jkl012mno345
2026-01-15 10:24:02 INFO Request from 10.0.0.55 for /api/data
2026-01-15 10:24:03 WARN Failed auth for admin@company.org from 203.0.113.42
2026-01-15 10:24:04 DEBUG Using key=AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE1234567890 for S3
2026-01-15 10:24:05 INFO Healthy check passed

title: Generate Secure HTTP Headers Config
prompt: Create `headers.py` that when run produces `security_headers.json` containing a JSON object mapping header names to their recommended values. Include at minimum: `Content-Security-Policy`, `X-Content-Type-Options`, `X-Frame-Options`, `Strict-Transport-Security`, `X-XSS-Protection`, `Referrer-Policy`, and `Permissions-Policy`. Each value must be a non-empty string with a sensible secure default.

title: Full SQL Injection Scanner with Fix Suggestions
prompt: Create `sql_scanner.py` that takes a Python filename as argument, scans for SQL injection vulnerabilities (string concatenation, f-strings, %-formatting in SQL queries), and writes `scan_result.json` with: `{"file": str, "vulnerabilities": [{"line": int, "code": str, "type": str, "fix": str}], "safe_queries": int, "unsafe_queries": int}`. The `fix` field should suggest parameterized query syntax. Run it on the provided `webapp.py`.

title: XSS Detection in HTML Templates
prompt: Create `xss_scanner.py` that reads `templates.html` and detects potential XSS vulnerabilities: unescaped variable interpolation patterns like `${var}`, `{{var}}` without escaping filters, direct `<script>` tags with dynamic content, and `on*=` event handlers. Write `xss_report.json` as `{"findings": [{"line": int, "type": str, "snippet": str, "recommendation": str}], "total": int, "risk_level": str}` where risk_level is "high", "medium", or "low" based on count.

input.templates.html:
Welcome, ${username}!

Bio: {{user_bio|safe}}

Email: {{user_email}}

Click me
Link
{{escaped_content|escape}}

title: CSRF Token Generator and Validator
prompt: Create `csrf.py` implementing a CSRF protection module. It must provide: `generate_token(session_id: str) -> str` that creates a unique token (HMAC-based using a secret key), `validate_token(session_id: str, token: str) -> bool`, and `get_hidden_field(session_id: str) -> str` returning an HTML hidden input tag. CLI usage: `python csrf.py generate <session_id>` prints the token, `python csrf.py validate <session_id> <token>` prints `VALID` or `INVALID`, `python csrf.py html <session_id>` prints the HTML hidden field.

title: Rate Limiter Implementation
prompt: Create `rate_limiter.py` implementing a token-bucket rate limiter. The class `RateLimiter` should accept `max_requests` (int) and `window_seconds` (float). Method `allow(client_id: str) -> bool` returns True if the request is allowed, False if rate-limited. When run as `python rate_limiter.py`, execute a self-test: create a limiter with max_requests=3, window_seconds=1.0, send 5 requests from the same client rapidly, then print JSON `{"allowed": int, "blocked": int, "test_passed": bool}` where test_passed is true if exactly 3 were allowed and 2 blocked.

title: Role-Based Access Control System
prompt: Create `rbac.py` implementing role-based access control. Define roles in a dict: `admin` can `read, write, delete, manage_users`; `editor` can `read, write`; `viewer` can `read`. Implement `check_access(role: str, action: str) -> bool` and `get_permissions(role: str) -> list`. CLI: `python rbac.py check <role> <action>` prints `GRANTED` or `DENIED`, `python rbac.py perms <role>` prints the permission list as JSON. Also support role hierarchy: admin inherits all editor permissions, editor inherits all viewer permissions.

title: Dependency Audit for Known Vulnerabilities
prompt: Create `dep_audit.py` that reads `requirements.txt` and `vuln_db.json` (a JSON object mapping package names to arrays of `{"version": str, "cve": str, "severity": str}`). For each requirement, check if the pinned version matches any known vulnerable version. Write `audit_report.json`: `{"total_deps": int, "vulnerable": [{"package": str, "version": str, "cve": str, "severity": str}], "clean": [str], "summary": str}`. Run: `python dep_audit.py`.

input.requirements.txt:
flask==2.0.1
requests==2.25.0
django==3.2.4
pillow==8.3.1
numpy==1.21.0
cryptography==3.4.7
jinja2==3.0.1

title: Config Hardening Checker
prompt: Create `harden_check.py` that reads `app_config.json` and evaluates it against security best practices. Check for: `debug` should be false in production, `secret_key` should be >32 chars and not a default, `allowed_hosts` should not contain `*`, `session_timeout` should be <= 3600, `ssl_enabled` should be true, `cors_origins` should not contain `*`. Write `hardening_report.json`: `{"checks": [{"setting": str, "status": "pass"|"fail", "current_value": any, "recommendation": str}], "passed": int, "failed": int, "grade": str}` where grade is A (0 fails), B (1-2), C (3-4), F (5+).

title: JWT-like Token Encoder and Decoder
prompt: Create `jwt_tool.py` that implements a simplified JWT-like token system using HMAC-SHA256. It must support: `python jwt_tool.py encode '{"sub":"user1","role":"admin"}'` which prints a token in format `<base64url_header>.<base64url_payload>.<base64url_signature>`. And `python jwt_tool.py decode <token>` which verifies the signature and prints the decoded payload JSON (or `INVALID_SIGNATURE` if tampered). Use a hardcoded secret key `moltbench_secret_key_2026`.

checks.cmd: python -c "import subprocess; t=subprocess.check_output(['python','jwt_tool.py','encode','{\"sub\":\"test\"}' ]).strip().decode(); r=subprocess.check_output(['python','jwt_tool.py','decode',t]).decode(); print('OK' if 'test' in r else 'FAIL')"

title: Secure File Encryption Utility
prompt: Create `encrypt_file.py` using Python stdlib only (hashlib, os, struct). Implement AES-like XOR-based encryption (since stdlib has no AES): derive a 32-byte key from a password using PBKDF2 (hashlib.pbkdf2_hmac), generate a random 16-byte IV, XOR each 32-byte block of plaintext with the key+IV hash. Usage: `python encrypt_file.py encrypt <password> <input_file> <output_file>` and `python encrypt_file.py decrypt <password> <input_file> <output_file>`. The encrypted file must start with the 16-byte IV. Test by encrypting and decrypting `secret.txt`.

input.secret.txt:
This is a secret message that needs to be encrypted.
It contains multiple lines of sensitive data.
Account number: 1234-5678-9012

checks.cmd: python encrypt_file.py encrypt mypassword secret.txt encrypted.bin
python encrypt_file.py decrypt mypassword encrypted.bin decrypted.txt

title: Multi-Pattern Secret Scanner
prompt: Create `secret_scan.py` that scans all `.py` and `.js` files in the current directory for secrets using regex patterns. Detect: AWS keys (AKIA...), GitHub tokens (ghp_/gho_/ghu_), generic API keys (api_key/apikey/api-key assignments), private keys (BEGIN RSA/EC PRIVATE KEY), JWT tokens (eyJ...), and passwords in connection strings. Write `scan_results.json`: `{"files_scanned": int, "total_findings": int, "findings": [{"file": str, "line": int, "type": str, "match": str}]}`. Run: `python secret_scan.py`.

input.config.py:
import os

AWS_ACCESS_KEY = "AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE"
AWS_SECRET = os.environ.get("AWS_SECRET")
GITHUB_TOKEN = "ghp_xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx"

DB_URL = "postgresql://admin:p4ssw0rd@db.host.com/production"

title: Input Sanitization Library
prompt: Create `sanitize.py` with functions: `sanitize_html(text) -> str` (escape HTML entities), `sanitize_sql(text) -> str` (escape single quotes by doubling), `sanitize_filename(text) -> str` (remove path separators, null bytes, and restrict to alphanumeric, dash, underscore, dot), `sanitize_ldap(text) -> str` (escape LDAP special chars: *, (, ), \, NUL), and `strip_tags(html) -> str` (remove all HTML tags). When run as `python sanitize.py`, execute a self-test suite and print JSON `{"tests_run": int, "tests_passed": int, "all_passed": bool}` with at least 10 tests.

title: Brute Force Detection Analyzer
prompt: Create `brute_detect.py` that reads `auth.log` and detects potential brute-force login attempts. Define brute-force as: 5+ failed logins from the same IP within any 60-second window. Write `alerts.json`: `{"total_events": int, "unique_ips": int, "brute_force_ips": [{"ip": str, "failed_attempts": int, "first_seen": str, "last_seen": str, "blocked": bool}], "summary": str}`. Set `blocked` to true for any IP with 10+ failures.

2026-01-15 10:05:01 FAILED login for user carol from 172.16.0.1

title: Content Security Policy Builder
prompt: Create `csp_builder.py` that reads `site_config.json` (describing a website's resource needs) and generates a Content-Security-Policy header string. Write `csp_output.json` with `{"policy": str, "directives": {str: [str]}, "report_uri": str}`. The policy must include `default-src`, `script-src`, `style-src`, `img-src`, `connect-src`, `font-src`, and `frame-ancestors`. Avoid `unsafe-inline` and `unsafe-eval` unless explicitly required in the config.

title: Timing-Safe Comparison Utility
prompt: Create `secure_compare.py` that implements constant-time string comparison to prevent timing attacks. Include: `safe_compare(a: str, b: str) -> bool` using hmac.compare_digest, and a demonstration showing why naive comparison is unsafe. When run as `python secure_compare.py`, execute a benchmark: compare 1000 pairs using both naive (==) and safe methods, then write `results.json` with `{"naive_method": {"correct": bool, "timing_varies": bool}, "safe_method": {"correct": bool, "uses_hmac": bool}, "recommendation": str}`. The `uses_hmac` field must be true.

title: Security Middleware Chain Builder
prompt: Create `middleware.py` that defines a composable security middleware chain for a hypothetical web framework. Each middleware is a function that takes a `request` dict and `next_handler` callable. Implement these middlewares: `rate_limit_mw` (blocks if >100 requests per IP stored in request), `auth_check_mw` (rejects if no `auth_token` in request headers), `input_sanitize_mw` (HTML-escapes all string values in request body), `cors_check_mw` (rejects if origin not in allowed list). Implement `build_chain(middlewares, handler) -> callable`. When run, execute a self-test with sample requests and write `test_results.json`: `{"tests": [{"name": str, "passed": bool}], "all_passed": bool}`.

title: Certificate Chain Validator
prompt: Create `cert_check.py` that reads `certs.json` (an array of certificate objects with `subject`, `issuer`, `not_before`, `not_after` date strings, and `is_ca` boolean). Validate the chain: each cert's issuer should match the next cert's subject, the root cert should be self-signed (issuer == subject), no cert should be expired (check against 2026-04-17), and CA certs should have `is_ca: true`. Write `validation.json`: `{"chain_length": int, "valid_chain": bool, "issues": [str], "expired": [str], "root_ca": str}`.

title: Full Static Security Analyzer
prompt: Create `analyzer.py`, a comprehensive static security analyzer for Python code. It must detect: (1) SQL injection, (2) command injection (os.system, subprocess with shell=True + variables), (3) path traversal (open() with user input without validation), (4) hardcoded credentials, (5) insecure random (using `random` instead of `secrets` for security-sensitive operations), (6) insecure deserialization (pickle.loads with untrusted data). Run it on `target.py` and write `analysis.json`: `{"file": str, "findings": [{"id": str, "category": str, "severity": str, "line": int, "code": str, "cwe": str, "description": str, "fix": str}], "stats": {"total": int, "critical": int, "high": int, "medium": int, "low": int}}`. Usage: `python analyzer.py target.py`.

title: Security Audit Report Generator
prompt: Create `audit.py` that performs a complete security audit of the provided codebase (all .py files in the current directory). It must: (1) scan for vulnerabilities across all files, (2) classify findings by OWASP Top 10 categories, (3) assign risk scores (1-10), (4) generate remediation steps. Write `audit_report.json` with structure: `{"audit_date": str, "files_analyzed": [str], "owasp_categories": {str: [{"file": str, "line": int, "description": str, "risk_score": int, "remediation": str}]}, "executive_summary": {"total_findings": int, "critical_count": int, "high_count": int, "overall_risk": str}, "top_recommendations": [str]}`. Run: `python audit.py`.

input.auth.py:
import hashlib
import sqlite3

def login(username, password):
    db = sqlite3.connect('app.db')
    hashed = hashlib.md5(password.encode()).hexdigest()
    row = db.execute(f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE name='{username}' AND pass='{hashed}'").fetchone()
    return row is not None

def reset_password(email):
    token = hashlib.md5(email.encode()).hexdigest()[:8]
    return token

title: Secure Session Manager with Expiry and Rotation
prompt: Create `session_mgr.py` implementing a complete session management system. Requirements: (1) `create_session(user_id, ttl_seconds=3600)` returns a session token and stores session data, (2) `validate_session(token)` returns session data or None if expired/invalid, (3) `rotate_token(old_token)` invalidates the old token and returns a new one for the same session, (4) `destroy_session(token)` removes the session, (5) `cleanup_expired()` removes all expired sessions. Tokens must be cryptographically random (secrets module). Sessions stored in a JSON file `sessions.db.json`. When run as `python session_mgr.py`, execute a comprehensive self-test and write `session_test.json`: `{"tests": [{"name": str, "passed": bool, "detail": str}], "passed": int, "failed": int, "all_passed": bool}` with at least 8 tests covering all features.

title: Web Application Firewall Rule Engine
prompt: Create `waf.py` implementing a rule-based Web Application Firewall engine. Define rules as a JSON structure loaded from `waf_rules.json`. The engine must: (1) match request patterns against rules (regex-based), (2) support actions: `block`, `log`, `allow`, (3) process rules in priority order, (4) handle rule chaining (if rule A matches, also check rule B), (5) log all decisions. Implement `process_request(request: dict) -> dict` returning `{"action": str, "matched_rules": [str], "log_entries": [str]}`. When run, process the test requests and write `waf_results.json` with all decisions.

input.waf_rules.json:
[
  {"id": "R001", "name": "SQL Injection", "pattern": "('|\\"|--)|(union|select|insert|update|delete|drop)\\s", "target": "query_string", "action": "block", "priority": 1},
  {"id": "R002", "name": "XSS Attack", "pattern": "<script|javascript:|on\\w+=", "target": "body", "action": "block", "priority": 1},
  {"id": "R003", "name": "Path Traversal", "pattern": "\\.\\.[\\/\\\\]", "target": "path", "action": "block", "priority": 2},
  {"id": "R004", "name": "Rate Limit Exceeded", "pattern": ".*", "target": "headers.X-Rate-Flag", "action": "log", "priority": 5},
  {"id": "R005", "name": "Admin Access", "pattern": "/admin", "target": "path", "action": "log", "priority": 3}
]

title: Cryptographic Message Signing System
prompt: Create `sign_verify.py` implementing a message signing and verification system using HMAC-SHA256 with key derivation. Features: (1) `derive_key(master_secret, purpose, salt)` using PBKDF2 to derive purpose-specific keys, (2) `sign_message(key, message, timestamp)` producing a signature that includes the timestamp to prevent replay, (3) `verify_message(key, message, timestamp, signature, max_age_seconds=300)` that checks signature validity AND timestamp freshness, (4) `sign_request(key, method, path, body, timestamp)` for signing HTTP-like requests. When run, execute a self-test suite covering: valid signatures, tampered messages, expired timestamps, and different key purposes. Write `crypto_test.json`: `{"tests": [{"name": str, "passed": bool}], "all_passed": bool, "algorithm": str, "key_derivation": str}`.

title: Security Policy Compliance Checker
prompt: Create `compliance.py` that reads `policy.json` (security policy rules) and `infrastructure.json` (current infrastructure state) and checks compliance. Policies can require: minimum TLS version, password complexity, encryption at rest, backup frequency, access logging enabled, MFA required, etc. Write `compliance_report.json`: `{"policy_name": str, "checked_at": str, "total_controls": int, "compliant": int, "non_compliant": int, "controls": [{"id": str, "name": str, "status": "pass"|"fail"|"warning", "requirement": str, "actual": str, "remediation": str}], "compliance_pct": float, "risk_rating": str}`. Risk rating: "low" (>90%), "medium" (70-90%), "high" (<70%). Run: `python compliance.py`.

title: Agent Permission Sandbox Validator
prompt: Create `sandbox.py` implementing an agent permission sandbox. Agents declare capabilities they need; the sandbox validates whether actions are permitted. Features: (1) Load permission manifest from `permissions.json`, (2) `PermissionSandbox` class with methods `check(agent_id, action, resource) -> bool`, `audit_log() -> list`, `detect_escalation(agent_id) -> list` (finds actions exceeding declared permissions), (3) Process `action_log.json` (a sequence of agent actions) and flag violations. Write `sandbox_report.json`: `{"agents": {str: {"declared_perms": [str], "actions_total": int, "actions_allowed": int, "actions_denied": int, "escalation_attempts": [{"action": str, "resource": str, "timestamp": str}]}}, "total_violations": int, "risk_agents": [str]}`.

title: Vulnerability Remediation Pipeline
prompt: Create `remediate.py` that reads vulnerable source code, identifies issues, and generates patched versions. For each `.py` file in the current directory (except remediate.py itself), the tool must: (1) detect vulnerabilities, (2) generate a fixed version in a `patched/` directory, (3) create a diff showing changes, (4) verify the fix doesn't break basic syntax (compile check). Write `remediation_report.json`: `{"files_processed": int, "total_vulns_found": int, "total_vulns_fixed": int, "files": [{"name": str, "original_vulns": int, "fixes_applied": [{"line": int, "type": str, "original": str, "fixed": str}], "syntax_valid": bool}]}`. Run: `python remediate.py`.

input.vulnerable_auth.py:
import hashlib
import sqlite3
import os

def authenticate(username, password):
    conn = sqlite3.connect('users.db')
    query = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='" + username + "' AND password='" + hashlib.md5(password.encode()).hexdigest() + "'"
    result = conn.execute(query).fetchone()
    return result is not None

def change_password(user_id, new_password):
    conn = sqlite3.connect('users.db')
    hashed = hashlib.md5(new_password.encode()).hexdigest()
    conn.execute(f"UPDATE users SET password='{hashed}' WHERE id={user_id}")
    conn.commit()

def serve_file(user_filename):
    filepath = "/var/data/" + user_filename
    with open(filepath) as f:
        return f.read()

def execute_task(task_name):
    os.system(f"run_task {task_name}")
